title: The Revival of National Statehood of Kazakhstan and the Development of the Country
overview: Lecture 9 covers the revival of Kazakh statehood after the dissolution of the USSR: declaration of independence (16 December 1991), delimitation and demarcation of state borders with all neighbors, transition challenges of the 1990s (hyperinflation, mass unemployment, collapse of agriculture, Aral disaster), political reforms (1995 Constitution, bicameral parliament, judicial reform, constitutional reforms of 2007/2017/2022), economic reforms (market transition, tenge 1993, mass privatization, Tengiz/Karachaganak/Kashagan oil fields, pipelines CPC and Atasu–Alashankou), social reforms (accumulative pension, Bologna higher-education system, 'Bolashak' programme, mandatory health insurance, Cultural Heritage, Digital Kazakhstan, Astana Hub), and the concept of 'Әділетті Қазақстан / Just Kazakhstan' under President Tokayev — built on social, economic, legal and political justice.

coreConcepts.definition: Constitutional Law 'On the State Independence of the Republic of Kazakhstan' adopted on 16 December 1991.
coreConcepts.example: Kazakhstan was the last republic to leave the USSR; Nazarbayev became the first President.

coreConcepts.definition: Delimitation = legal definition of the border line on maps; demarcation = physical marking on the ground.
coreConcepts.example: Bilateral agreements signed with Russia, China, Kyrgyzstan, Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan; ~13,000 km of state border secured.

coreConcepts.definition: New Constitution adopted by nationwide referendum on 30 August 1995; established a presidential republic with a bicameral parliament (Majilis + Senate).
coreConcepts.example: Replaced the transitional 1993 Constitution; Constitution Day = 30 August.

coreConcepts.definition: National currency introduced on 15 November 1993, creating an independent financial system.
coreConcepts.example: Initial rate: 1 USD ≈ 4.7 tenge; ended the rouble-zone dependency.

coreConcepts.definition: Transfer of state-owned property into private hands during 1991–1998 to build a market economy.
coreConcepts.example: Coupon privatization phase; sale of large industrial assets to strategic investors; rise of the private sector.

coreConcepts.definition: Capital moved from Almaty to Akmola (renamed Astana) — decision 1994, law 1995, official transfer 10 December 1997, international presentation 10 June 1998.
coreConcepts.example: Astana → Nur-Sultan (2019) → Astana (2022). Symbols: Bayterek, Khan Shatyr, Library of the First President.

coreConcepts.definition: Presidential scholarship (est. 1993) sending top Kazakhstani students to leading universities worldwide.
coreConcepts.example: Thousands of 'Bolashak' alumni now staff ministries, universities, and major companies.

coreConcepts.definition: Mass protests in January 2022 sparked by a sharp rise in liquefied-gas prices in Zhanaozen, spreading nationwide.
coreConcepts.example: 2–11 January; state of emergency; ~238 killed; CSTO peacekeepers invited; Nazarbayev stripped of 'Elbasy' status.

coreConcepts.definition: President Tokayev's political concept of building a fair, equitable society based on social, economic, legal and political justice.
coreConcepts.example: Equal opportunities, fair wealth distribution, equality before the law, transparent governance, civic participation.

coreConcepts.definition: Constitutional amendments approved by referendum on 5 June 2022; introduced a single 7-year presidential term, strengthened parliament and local self-government.
coreConcepts.example: Akims of districts and rural areas elected; presidential relatives barred from senior state posts.

expandedNotes.heading: Revival of Statehood & National Independence
expandedNotes.body: Following the dissolution of the USSR in 1991, Kazakhstan declared independence on 16 December 1991. In March 1992 the Republic of Kazakhstan became a full member of the United Nations. In 1993 the first Constitution of Kazakhstan was adopted, laying the foundations of the state system and enshrining the fundamental rights of citizens. Key institutions were formed — parliament, government, judiciary — and administrative-division reforms were carried out.

expandedNotes.heading: Defining the State Borders
expandedNotes.body: The declaration of independence created a priority strategic need for clear borders. International recognition required recognition of borders. Kazakhstan signed bilateral agreements with Russia, China, Kyrgyzstan, Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan. DELIMITATION (defining the precise course of the border on maps) and DEMARCATION (setting up border markers on the ground) ensured clear designation and agreement with neighbouring states. HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE: confirmed sovereignty, secured territorial integrity, prevented disputes, strengthened peace and stability in Central Asia, formed national identity, enabled foreign trade, ensured national security against smuggling and illegal migration.

expandedNotes.heading: Challenges of the Transition Period
expandedNotes.body: After the collapse of the USSR new state institutions had to be built under political instability. Transition to a market economy caused a collapse of production, hyperinflation (1993) and mass unemployment. Closure of state and collective farms triggered a deep crisis in agriculture; productivity collapsed for lack of investment and modern technology. Mass rural-to-urban migration created housing/infrastructure shortages; brain drain abroad reduced the skilled workforce. The drying of the Aral Sea worsened the ecological situation, and Soviet-era industrial plants continued to pollute. Underfunding of science, education and culture led to institutional decline. By the 2000s, oil dependence, regional inequality, slow agricultural modernization and high corruption remained key challenges.

expandedNotes.heading: Political Reforms
expandedNotes.body: 1995 Constitution (referendum, 30 August) — strong presidency, separation of powers, bicameral parliament: Majilis (lower house) and Senate (upper house) — passes budget, ratifies treaties, legislates. JUDICIAL REFORM: independent courts; Supreme Court as the highest body; professional judiciary. POLITICAL LIBERALIZATION: expanded citizens' rights, NGOs, media. CONSTITUTIONAL REFORM 2007: expanded parliamentary powers, role of parties, election of akims at the local level. CONSTITUTIONAL REFORM 2017: redistribution of powers from president to government/parliament; stronger checks and balances. CONSTITUTIONAL REFORM 2022: single 7-year presidential term, no re-election; elected akims at district and rural level; expanded local self-government.

expandedNotes.heading: Economic Reforms
expandedNotes.body: TRANSITION TO MARKET: price liberalization, demonopolization, introduction of TENGE (15 Nov 1993), modern commercial banking. PRIVATIZATION: mass privatization, development of SMEs. ENERGY SECTOR: development of Tengiz, Karachaganak and Kashagan; attraction of major foreign investors (Chevron, ExxonMobil, ENI, Total). EXPORT PIPELINES: Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC) and Atasu–Alashankou pipeline to China. INSTITUTIONAL REFORMS: property law, business regulation. INFRASTRUCTURE: highways (Astana–Borovoye, Astana–Pavlodar, Almaty–Karaganda–Astana), railways (Aksu–Sary; Altynsarin–Derzhavino; Zhezkazgan–Beineu), airports. DIVERSIFICATION: State Programme of Industrial-Innovative Development since 2010; non-resource sectors, IT, tax incentives, special economic zones. OUTCOME: foundation for sustainable growth and rising standards of living.

expandedNotes.heading: Social Reforms
expandedNotes.body: Kazakhstan is constitutionally a SOCIAL STATE. PENSION REFORM 1998: accumulative pension system (the first in the CIS); regular indexation; social support to low-income families, mothers with many children, persons with disabilities; affordable housing programmes. EDUCATION: Bologna 3-tier system (bachelor/master/PhD); '100 Schools' and 'Comfortable School' modernization; national, international, private universities; free vocational education; new standards and digitization; e-textbooks and distance learning. BOLASHAK programme — international training. HEALTHCARE: modernization of hospitals, primary care network, professional training, e-medical records, MANDATORY HEALTH INSURANCE, disease-prevention programmes. CULTURAL REVIVAL: 'Cultural Heritage' programme; international festivals. SPORTS: stadiums, 'Artsport', Olympic movement. DIGITAL KAZAKHSTAN: e-government, online services, automation, internet access, ASTANA HUB technopark and start-up ecosystem.

expandedNotes.heading: Transfer of the Capital
expandedNotes.body: 1994: Parliament approved the move. 6 July 1994 — law. 10 December 1997 — official transfer. 10 June 1998 — international presentation. Akmola renamed Astana in 1998; renamed Nur-Sultan 23 March 2019 in honour of Nazarbayev's resignation; restored as Astana in September 2022. Key landmarks: Bayterek tower, Khan Shatyr, Library of the First President, the new diplomatic district.

expandedNotes.heading: Power Transition & Qantar (2022)
expandedNotes.body: 19 March 2019 — Nazarbayev announced resignation. Kassym-Jomart Tokayev sworn in as acting President; won presidential elections on 9 June 2019. JANUARY 2022 (QANTAR): protests in Zhanaozen against the rise of LPG prices spread nationwide; in Almaty turned to looting and an attempt to seize the airport. 5 January — Tokayev requested CSTO peacekeepers; order restored by mid-January; ~238 killed. AFTERMATH: Nazarbayev lost the 'Elbasy' status and key state posts; reforms under the 'Listening State' doctrine. 5 June 2022 — new Constitution by referendum. Tokayev re-elected for a single 7-year term in November 2022 elections.

expandedNotes.heading: The Concept of 'Just Kazakhstan' (Әділетті Қазақстан)
expandedNotes.body: A political concept by President Tokayev aimed at building a fair, equitable society. KEY PRINCIPLES: (1) SOCIAL JUSTICE — equal opportunities regardless of origin/ethnicity/religion; effective social protection. (2) ECONOMIC JUSTICE — fair distribution of national wealth; support for SMEs. (3) LEGAL JUSTICE — equality before the law; independent judiciary; anti-corruption; protection of human rights; civil society institutions. (4) POLITICAL JUSTICE — transparent and accountable government; democratic institutions; civic participation; local self-government. GOALS: improving quality of life (better education, healthcare, housing), sustainable and diversified economic growth, strengthening national unity and interethnic/interfaith harmony, modernizing state institutions to be more efficient and transparent.

keyScholars.name: Nursultan Nazarbayev
keyScholars.contribution: First President of Kazakhstan (1991–2019); architect of the '2030' and '2050' strategies; led nuclear disarmament, the move of the capital, and the introduction of the tenge.

keyScholars.name: Kassym-Jomart Tokayev
keyScholars.contribution: Second President of Kazakhstan (from 2019); career diplomat (former UN Under-Secretary-General); architect of the 'New Kazakhstan' and 'Just Kazakhstan' concepts, 2022 Constitution.

keyScholars.name: Shaken Niyazbekov
keyScholars.contribution: Designer of the State Flag of Kazakhstan (adopted 4 June 1992).

keyScholars.name: Zhandarbek Malibekov
keyScholars.contribution: Co-designer of the State Emblem of Kazakhstan.

keyScholars.name: Shota-Aman Walikhanov
keyScholars.contribution: Co-designer of the State Emblem of Kazakhstan.

comparativeTables.title: Constitutional Reforms of Independent Kazakhstan
rows: Foundations of the state system; rights of citizens
Expanded parliamentary powers; election of akims at local level
Redistribution of powers; stronger checks and balances
Single 7-year presidential term; elected akims of districts/rural areas

comparativeTables.title: Major Oil & Gas Fields and Export Routes
rows: One of the world's largest; developed with Chevron
Major source of gas and condensate
Largest discovery of the late 20th century

realWorldExamples: 16 December — Independence Day of Kazakhstan; main state holiday.
Bayterek tower (1997) — symbol of Astana and the new statehood.
EXPO-2017 in Astana — theme 'Future Energy'; first EXPO in Central Asia.
Bolashak alumni hold leading positions in government, business and academia.
Astana Hub — the largest IT start-up technopark in Central Asia.
5 June 2022 referendum — over 77% supported the new constitutional amendments.

timeline.event: Constitutional Law on State Independence of the Republic of Kazakhstan
Kazakhstan admitted to the United Nations
Parliament approves the transfer of the capital
Official transfer of the capital to Akmola
Accumulative pension system introduced (first in CIS)
Constitutional reform — stronger parliament; election of local akims
State Programme of Industrial-Innovative Development launched
Nazarbayev resigns; Tokayev becomes acting President
Qantar — January events; CSTO peacekeepers invited
Tokayev re-elected for a single 7-year term

mustMemorize: 10 December 1997 — official transfer of the capital to Akmola/Astana
1998 — accumulative pension system, first in the CIS
Just Kazakhstan — Tokayev's concept: social, economic, legal, political justice
cheatSheet:
1991 Independence → 1992 UN + symbols → 1993 first Constitution + tenge → 1995 current Constitution → 1997 capital → 1998 pension reform → 2017 EXPO → 2019 Tokayev → 2022 Qantar + new Constitution
Main fields: Tengiz, Karachaganak, Kashagan | Pipelines: CPC, Atasu–Alashankou
Reforms: market transition + privatization + Bologna + Bolashak + Digital Kazakhstan + Just Kazakhstan
mnemonics: T-K-K — Tengiz, Karachaganak, Kashagan (the three oil giants)
M & S — Majilis (lower) + Senate (upper) = bicameral parliament
4 J's of 'Just Kazakhstan': Social, Economic, Legal, Political Justice
